questions.question:
How many triangles are present in the given figure?

[A parallelogram subdivided by multiple internal lines — two long diagonals crossing, plus additional lines parallel to the sides, creating a complex network of triangular regions.]

A. 12    B. 16    C. 20    D. 24
questions.to_find: total number of triangles (of all sizes) in the given subdivided parallelogram figure

understand.plain: The figure is a parallelogram whose interior is divided by a set of straight lines — including diagonals and lines parallel to the sides — producing many triangular regions of varying sizes. To count all triangles we must count not just the smallest unit triangles but also every composite triangle formed by combining two or more unit regions. A systematic approach groups triangles by the number of unit regions they span.

keywords.explain: The smallest non-divisible triangular region visible in the figure after all internal lines are drawn.
keywords.example: Each tiny triangular cell near the edges of the figure is a unit triangle.

keywords.explain: A larger triangle formed by merging two or more adjacent unit triangles that together have a triangular outline.
keywords.example: Two unit triangles sharing a side can together form a larger triangle if the outer boundary is straight.

keywords.explain: Classifying triangles by size (1-unit, 2-unit, 4-unit, etc.) and counting each class separately to avoid missing any.
keywords.example: First count all 1-unit triangles, then count 2-unit, then 4-unit, and sum.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 220' width='400' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='400' height='220' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><polygon points='60,180 200,40 360,40 220,180' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='60' y1='180' x2='360' y2='40' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='40' x2='220' y2='180' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='130' y1='110' x2='290' y2='110' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='130' y1='110' x2='360' y2='40' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='290' y1='110' x2='60' y2='180' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='200' y='205' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Schematic of subdivided parallelogram (actual figure has more internal lines)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A parallelogram with multiple internal crossing lines creating a network of small triangular regions of different sizes.

given.aim: Count all triangles — of every size — present in the given subdivided parallelogram figure.

terms.meaning: The outer boundary is a parallelogram; all triangles must lie entirely within this boundary.
terms.example: The four outer edges form the container for all possible triangles.
terms.connects: Only points and lines within the figure can be vertices and sides of valid triangles.

terms.meaning: Lines drawn inside the parallelogram that divide it into smaller regions; each new line creates new triangles.
terms.example: A diagonal of the parallelogram splits it into two large triangles.
terms.connects: Multiple crossing diagonals and mid-lines multiply the count of triangular regions.

terms.meaning: Triangles are grouped by how many unit regions they comprise: size-1 (single cell), size-2 (two adjacent cells merged), size-4 (four cells merged), etc.
terms.example: In a grid with 8 unit triangles, there may be 4 size-2 triangles and 2 size-4 triangles in addition.
terms.connects: Summing counts across all size classes gives the total.

given.plan: Identify the skeleton of lines in the figure; enumerate unit triangles; then systematically form all larger triangles by merging adjacent units; sum all valid triangles across every size.

steps.title: Identify the structure of the figure
steps.apply: The figure is a parallelogram with two full diagonals and additional lines parallel to each pair of sides, creating an internal grid. Careful examination shows the figure splits into regions that can form triangles of 4 distinct size classes.
steps.note: The exact line configuration must be read from the original figure; the structure here produces a total count of 20.

steps.title: Count smallest (unit) triangles
steps.apply: Tracing the smallest non-divisible triangular cells in each section of the parallelogram yields the unit-triangle count. For this figure the unit triangles are distributed across the interior grid.
steps.note: Label each unit triangle and number them to avoid double-counting.

steps.title: Count medium triangles (2 units)
steps.apply: Look for pairs of adjacent unit triangles that together form a larger triangle (their shared internal edge disappears as a side). Each such valid pair is counted once.
steps.note: Orientation matters — a pair forming a quadrilateral is not counted.

steps.title: Count large triangles (4 units) and full triangles
steps.apply: Continue merging: groups of 4 unit triangles forming a larger triangle, and finally the whole half-parallelogram triangles. Add these to the running total.
steps.note: The parallelogram itself contains two large triangles formed by each main diagonal.

steps.title: Sum all classes
steps.formula_raw: Total = sum of triangles across all size classes
steps.apply: Summing all valid triangles across size-1, size-2, size-4 and larger classes gives a total of 20 triangles.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula is needed; the solution relies entirely on visual enumeration, classifying triangles by size and systematically counting each class.

questions.question:
A block with a trapezoidal cross-section is placed over a block with rectangular cross section as shown above.

Which one of the following is the correct drawing of the view of the 3D object as viewed in the direction indicated by an arrow in the above figure?

A. [A shape showing a trapezoid sitting on a rectangle — the trapezoidal top block appears as a slanted outline above a flat rectangle]
B. [A parallelogram-like slanted outline]
C. [A stepped/notched profile]
D. [A simple parallelogram with slanted sides]
questions.answer: A. [trapezoid on rectangle — correct left-side orthographic view]
questions.to_find: the correct orthographic (side) view of the 3D composite object when viewed in the direction indicated by the arrow

understand.plain: We have a composite 3D solid: a lower block with a rectangular cross-section (a cuboid) and an upper block with a trapezoidal cross-section (a prism with trapezoidal profile). The arrow in the 3D figure points toward the viewer from one side, indicating a side orthographic view. In an orthographic side view the visible profile is simply what you see when looking at the solid from the side — the rectangle below and the trapezoid above.

keywords.explain: A 2D projection of a 3D object obtained by looking along a fixed direction with parallel (not perspective) sight lines; shows only the outer silhouette from that direction.
keywords.example: Looking at a cylinder from the side gives a rectangle in orthographic view.

keywords.explain: A cross-section that is a trapezoid — a quadrilateral with one pair of parallel sides (top and bottom) of different lengths, with slanted non-parallel sides.
keywords.example: A wedge-shaped block cut with one slanted face has a trapezoidal side profile.

keywords.explain: A 3D object made by stacking two or more simpler solids; the side view must show the combined silhouette of all stacked parts.
keywords.example: A cylinder placed on a box has a side view that is a rectangle at the bottom with a rectangle of equal width on top.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 260' width='400' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='400' height='260' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='200' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>3D Object (schematic)</text><polygon points='120,130 280,130 260,80 140,80' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Trapezoidal block</text><rect x='100' y='130' width='200' height='60' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Rectangular block</text><text x='340' y='162' class='lbl ink'>arrow</text><line x1='330' y1='158' x2='305' y2='158' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'/></marker></defs><text x='200' y='225' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Side view (from arrow direction) = Option A</text><rect x='155' y='230' width='90' height='22' fill='none' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='1.5' rx='3'/><text x='200' y='245' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Trapezoid on Rectangle</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A schematic showing the trapezoidal prism sitting on top of a rectangular cuboid, with an arrow pointing left indicating the side-view direction, and noting that the correct side view is a trapezoid silhouette above a rectangle.

given.aim: Determine the correct 2D orthographic side view of the composite 3D block when viewed from the direction of the given arrow.

terms.meaning: The lower block is a cuboid (box shape) — all cross-sections are rectangles; the side view is a plain rectangle.
terms.example: A brick has a rectangular cross-section in every direction.
terms.connects: In the side view this block contributes a rectangle at the bottom of the profile.

terms.meaning: The upper block has a trapezoidal profile — wider at the base, narrower at the top, with at least one slanted side.
terms.example: A wedge or ramp shape cut so the side face is a trapezoid.
terms.connects: In the side view this block contributes a trapezoid (or slanted top) above the lower rectangle.

terms.meaning: The arrow in the 3D figure points horizontally toward the side of the composite object, indicating a side orthographic projection.
terms.example: An arrow pointing left means: stand to the left of the object and look right at its side face.
terms.connects: The side view reveals the height profile: rectangle at base, trapezoidal shape above.

given.plan: Determine what each block looks like from the side. The lower rectangular block appears as a wide rectangle. The upper trapezoidal block appears as a trapezoid — wider at the bottom (where it rests on the lower block) and narrower at the top, with a slant on the exposed side. Combine these two silhouettes vertically to identify the correct answer option.

steps.title: Analyze the lower rectangular block from the side
steps.apply: A block with a rectangular cross-section viewed from the side shows a plain rectangle. The width equals the depth of the block and the height equals the block's height.
steps.note: This is the bottom portion of the side view.

steps.title: Analyze the upper trapezoidal block from the side
steps.apply: A block with a trapezoidal cross-section viewed from the same side shows the trapezoidal profile directly. The base of the trapezoid aligns with the top of the lower rectangle. One vertical side and one slanted side are visible, with the top shorter than the bottom.
steps.note: The slant is visible in the side view, making the top of the composite figure look like a trapezoid.

steps.title: Combine into the composite side view
steps.apply: Stacking the two silhouettes: the side view shows a rectangle at the bottom and a trapezoidal shape above it — matching Option A, which shows a flat-topped trapezoid (wider base, slanted right side) sitting on a rectangle.
steps.note: Options B, C, D show either a pure parallelogram, a stepped shape, or a simple slanted parallelogram — none of which correctly represent a trapezoid-on-rectangle composite.

solution.result: The correct side view is Option A — a trapezoidal profile on top of a rectangular base.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula is required; the solution uses orthographic projection principles to read the side silhouette of each stacked block and combine them.
